coredata.dc:title: A highly selective semiconducting polymer dots-based “off–on” fluorescent nanoprobe for iron, copper and histidine detection and imaging in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Chabok, Ammar
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Yeganeh-Faal, Ali
Molaabasi, Fatemeh
Molaei, Karam
Sarparast, Morteza
coredata.dc:description: Semiconducting polymer dots (PDs) hold a great promise as fluorescence nanoprobes, due to their photostability, biocompatibility, and high quantum yield. Herein, the synthesis and characterization of highly fluorescent PDs for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+,Cu2+, and histidine (His) have been reported. First, carboxyl functionalized poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1′,3)-thiadiazole)] (PFBT) PDs were synthesized through a nano-precipitation technique, and then they were functionalized by -COOH groups using 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid. The formation of PDs was proved using transmission electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering, and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analyses. The PDs exhibited a yellow fluorescence with a peak centered at 540 nm (photo-excited at 460 nm) with a quantum yield of 25%. The fluorescence of PDs significantly quenched in the presence of Cu2+ ion, and then selectively recovered upon addition of His, providing the possibility of constructing a sensitive Cu2+-His off-on fluorescent nanoprobe. The PDs exhibited a linear dynamic range for Cu2+ from 0.1 to 630 µmol L−1 with a limit of detection of 61.7 nmol L−1, and for Fe3+ from 0.1 to 720 µmol L−1 with a limit of detection of 58.1 nmol L−1. In addition, the PDs/Cu2+ probe showed a linear dynamic range for His from 0.1 to 920 µmol L−1 with a limit of detection of 79.6 nmol L−1. Besides, the prepared PDs/Cu2+ probe exhibited a promising potential for selective and sensitive sensing of His in blood serum and for intracellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Semiconducting polymer dots
Fluorescence
Off-on nanoprobe
Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions
Histidine
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid doped PFBT PDs
Sensing behavior of PDs towards metal ions
Recognition mechanism for off-on switching behavior of 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid @PFBT PDs
Application of COOH functionalized PFBT PDstohistidine-rich proteins, biological samples and living cells
Comparison of 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid doped PFBT PDs with previously reported fluorescent probes for detection of copper ion and His

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support of this work by Iran Science Elites Federation (ISEF) and Research Councils of Tarbiat Modares and Razi Universities is gratefully acknowledged. We also thank Payam Noor University, Qom Branch, for the laboratory support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), DLS histogram (b), Zeta potential of non-functionalized (c) Zeta potential of functionalized (d) and IR spectrum (e) of PDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption (black line), excitation (green line, emission at 540 nm) and fluorescence spectra (red line, excited at 460 nm) of PDs. Inset shows the photograph taken under 365 nm UV light. (b) Time-dependent FL intensity of PDs upon the exposure to 1.5 µmol L−1 Cu2+ for 1, 2, 5 and 10 min (c) Fluorescence response of PDs upon addition of various concentrations of Cu2+. (d) Fluorescence quenching of PDs as a function of Cu2+ concentration. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the PDs probe solution in the absence and in the presence of Cu2+, respectively. Inset shows plots of (F0-F)/F0 of PDs vs. Cu2+ ion concentrations (e) Fluorescence response of PDs upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+. (f) Fluorescence quenching of PDs as a function of Fe3+ concentration. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the PDs probe solution in the absence and in the presence of Fe3+, respectively. Inset shows plots of (F0-F)/F0 of PDs vs. Fe3+ ions concentrations. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 460 nm) of PDs in the presence of 200 µM of different metal ions. (b) Normalized FL intensities of PDs in the presence of different cations.

(a) Fluorescence responses of PDs/Cu2+ to addition of various concentrations of His. Inset shows the respective photographs of PDs/Cu2+ and PDs/Cu2+/His. (b) Plot of fluorescence enhancement as a function of His concentration. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of PDs/Cu2+ probe solution in the absence and in the presence of His, respectively. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 460 nm) of PDs/Cu2+ in the presence of different amino acids at a concentration of 100 μM. (d) Normalized FL intensities of PDs/Cu2+ in the presence of different tested amino acids.
TEM images of PDs (a), PDs/Cu2+ (c) and PDs/Cu2+/His (e), and the corresponding DLS measurements of PDs (b), PDs/Cu2+ (d) and PDs/Cu2+/His (f).
(a) Reversible fluorescence responses of PDs for 7 consecutive cycles. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of PDs/Cu2+ in the absence and presence of 2.0 equivalents of BSA.
Bright field, fluorescence and overlay imaging of MCF-7 cells stained with PDs (a, b, c), stained with PDs/Cu2+ (d, e, f), and stained with PDs/Cu2+/His (g, h, i).
Schematic illustration of preparation of PDs and their application to off-on Cu2+ and His sensing.

Results of recovery studies from mineral and tap water samples spiked with copper and iron ions by carboxyl functionalized PDs and AAS (A) and from human serum and plasma spiked withhistidine by carboxyl functionalized PDs and GC-MS (B) (n = 3).
Comparison of different sensor platforms for the detection of copper and iron ions and histidine.

entry.bold: Fluorescence probes for determination of Fe
Fluorescence probes for determination of Cu
Fluorescence probes for determination of His

head.ce:title: A highly selective semiconducting polymer dots-based “off–on” fluorescent nanoprobe for iron, copper and histidine detection and imaging in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Michigan State University, East Lansing, MI 48824-1322, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Michigan State University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Semiconducting polymer dots (PDs) hold a great promise as fluorescence nanoprobes, due to their photostability, biocompatibility, and high quantum yield. Herein, the synthesis and characterization of highly fluorescent PDs for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+,Cu2+, and histidine (His) have been reported. First, carboxyl functionalized poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1′,3)-thiadiazole)] (PFBT) PDs were synthesized through a nano-precipitation technique, and then they were functionalized by -COOH groups using 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid. The formation of PDs was proved using transmission electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering, and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analyses. The PDs exhibited a yellow fluorescence with a peak centered at 540 nm (photo-excited at 460 nm) with a quantum yield of 25%. The fluorescence of PDs significantly quenched in the presence of Cu2+ ion, and then selectively recovered upon addition of His, providing the possibility of constructing a sensitive Cu2+-His off-on fluorescent nanoprobe. The PDs exhibited a linear dynamic range for Cu2+ from 0.1 to 630 µmol L−1 with a limit of detection of 61.7 nmol L−1, and for Fe3+ from 0.1 to 720 µmol L−1 with a limit of detection of 58.1 nmol L−1. In addition, the PDs/Cu2+ probe showed a linear dynamic range for His from 0.1 to 920 µmol L−1 with a limit of detection of 79.6 nmol L−1. Besides, the prepared PDs/Cu2+ probe exhibited a promising potential for selective and sensitive sensing of His in blood serum and for intracellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Histidine (His) residues with side chains that can function either as a base or as an acid play a pivotal role in biological systems. By controlling transmission of metal ions, His acts as a neurotransmitter in mammalian central nervous system and, in some of proteins, helps the repair and growth of tissues [1–3]. However, the abnormal level of His in plasma is mostly a sign of diseases such as acute liver failure [4], kidney disease [5], and rheumatoid arthritis [6]. Besides, a long term His deficiency may lead to epilepsy, failure of normal erythropoiesis development, Parkinson's disease, and Friedreich ataxia [7]. Thus, the rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of His is important in clinical diagnostics. Thus far, a number of analytical methods including HPLC [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], electrochemical voltammetry [10], and UV–vis spectrophotometry [11,12] have been utilized for His determination, some of which still suffering from such obstacles as expensive facilities, time consuming, sophisticated instrumentation and high detection limits. Meanwhile, the fluorescence methods with the advantages of high selectivity and sensitivity, low detection limit, availability and simplicity of instrument exhibited promising potential for His sensing. However, finding a fluorescent probe with biocompatibility, high photostability, and extraordinary sensitivity and selectivity toward His is still a challenging task [13,14].

Similar to His, copper and iron ions are well known to play crucial roles in biological systems with different functions. For example, in living systems, copper ions act as cofactor of many metal-enzymes, which generating cellular energy, inducing signal transduction, and reducing the molecular oxygen [15]. However, if exceeded, they cause apoptosis and a series of neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson, Wilson, and Alzheimer diseases, via breaking of the cellular homeostasis [16,17]. Therefore, establishing of highly selective and sensitive approaches for copper and iron determination is of critical importance. Up to now, numerous strategies including inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), atomic emission/absorption spectroscopy (AES/AAS), and electrochemical methods have been put forward toward this goal; yet, majority of them are costly, complicated and required tedious pretreatments. Alternatively, the fluorescence based nanobiosensors offer excellent potential for rapid, low cost, and simple detection of copper and iron ions in biological and toxicological samples. Over recent years, a number of fluorescent probes have been designed for sensitive detection of Fe3+,Cu2+, and His [18–28].

Recently, semiconducting polymer nanoparticles, called also as polymer dots (PDs), have attracted great attention as a new class of fluorescent nanoprobes, because of their extraordinary brightness, high photostability, facile surface functionalization, fast radiative rate, and nontoxic properties [29–31]. PDs possess improved brightness and photostability compared to organic dyes, quantum dots, carbon dots and metallic nanoclusters. In fact, blinking and significant fraction of non-fluorescent dots together with low emission rates cause the quantum dots not to be bright enough; besides, their toxicity resulted from leaching of heavy metal ions imped their biological implementation. Likewise,the metallic nanoclusters suffer from low quantum yields and photostability [29,32–34]. Although the PDs do not have these limitations, they generally are hydrophobic and non-soluble in water, which limits their biological applications. However, converting the PDs with hydrophilic nanoparticles can occur via trapping of heterogeneous molecules inside a single PD during nanoparticle formation [29,35–37]. In spite of various exciting progresses achieved to bring PDs into clinical or practical applications, it is required to address some existing challenges. Infact, in this respect, several state-of-the-art applications of semiconducting PDs have already been developed [30,31,38].

In this work, we developed a simple method to functionalize poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1′,3)-thiadiazole)] (PFBT) PDs by -COOH groups using 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid. The resulting functionalized PDs selectively showed a strong aggregation induced florescence quenching in the presence of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, due to the chelating ability of -COOH groups present. In the next step, the fluorescence of functionalized PDs was found to recover by removal of Cu2+ from the PDs via addition of His to the medium. Thus, we interestingly designed a highly sensitive and selective “off–on” fluorescent nanoprobe for copper and histidine detection and for imaging in living cells. The synthetic procedures along with “off-on” fluorescent application of the functionalized PDs are illustrated in Scheme 1.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1′,3)-thiadiazole)] (PFBT, 10 kDa MW, polydispersity index PDI of 2.3), 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid, L-phenylalanine, NaCl, NaOH, Cu(NO3)2, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4 and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). L-arginine, L-glutamine, L-alanine, L-cysteine, L-aspartic acid, L-asparagine, L-lysine, L-leucine, L-serine, L-glycine, L-histidine, L-tyrosine, L-threonine, D-methionine, D-tryptophan, and D-valine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company (St. Louis, MO). Li2SO4, KCl, NaCl, NaOH, BaCl2, CaCl2, FeCl2, HgCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, NiCl2, SnCl2, SrCl2, ZrOCl2, AgNO3, AlCl3, Co(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, FeCl3, Ce(NO3)3, Cr(NO3)3, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, sodium citrate and L-phenylalanine were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Doubly distilled deionized water was used throughout.

Spectrofluorometric measurements were carried out using an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a xenon lamp as the excitation light source (Agilent Technologies, US). The emission spectra were recorded over the wavelength range of 500–700 nm upon excitation at 460 nm at a scan rate of 1500 nm min−1. The spectral band passesfor excitation and emission were set at 10 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu model UV 2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu,Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on an EM-10C Zeiss transmission electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany) with an accelerating voltage of 150 kV. The Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were obtained using a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument and sub-micrometer particle size analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The fluorescence images were obtained using a Nikon Eclipse TE2000-S apparatus (Nikon Instruments, Japan). An ultrasonic bath (Starsonic 90, Liarre, Italy) was used for sonication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid doped PFBT PDs
ce:section.ce:para: The functionalized PDs were prepared by a nano-precipitation technique as described elsewhere [39] and shown in Scheme 1. Briefly, first, 1.0 mgmL−1 solutions of both PFBT and 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid were prepared in THF, as stock solutions. Then, 250 µL of PFBT and 50 µL of 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid solutions were added to 5 mL THF and mixed well. After that, the resultant mixture was immediately injected into 10 mL of distilled water under sonication for 2 min, at room temperature. Finally, THF was removed by N2 bubbling on an 80 °C hotplate, followed by filtration through a 0.2 µm filter. The synthesized PDs were then characterized by TEM, FTIR and DLS and zeta potential measurements.

The relative fluorescence quantum yield of the PDs was determined using the following established equation:
QY
s
=
Q
Yr
(
I
s
I
r
)
(
A
r
A
x
)
(
η
s
2
η
s
2
)
where r and s refer to the standard reference (fluorescein) and the PDs respectively, Q is the quantum yield, I is the integrated emission spectra, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the absorbance, where it was kept below 0.1.

30 µL of PDs and 2.0 mL of phosphate buffer (10 mmol L−1, pH 7.4) were mixed and transferred into a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette. The fluorescence spectra were recorded over the range of 500–700 nm upon excitation at 460 nm. The excitation and emission slits were both set at 10 nm. Different concentrations of Cu2+or Fe3+ were successively added to the cuvette to reach a final concentration of 1200 µmol L−1 with 2 min equilibrium time. After each addition, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded at room temperature.

For His detection, the fluorescence of PDs/Cu2+nanoprobe was measured in response to the successive additions of His. After each addition, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded at a 460 nm excitation wavelength. The selectivity of the fluorescence assay was investigated in detail, in order to study the interference effects of various amino acids on His detection.

To eliminate the matrix effects, standard addition method was performed. For His detection, the blood serum samples were collected from healthy volunteers, instantly frozen, and then stored at −20 °C until analysis. Before starting the assay, the serum samples (0.5 mL) were diluted with acetonitrile (3 mL) and vortexed for 30 s to deposit the proteins present, and afterward, incubated for 10 min in ice. Finally, before doing analysis, the deproteinized serums were appropriately collected by centrifuging the prepared samples for 15 min at 13,000 rpm at 4 °C. All fluorescence measurements were carried out at room temperature under ambient conditions.

The MCF-7 cells were obtained and cultured in the exponential phase of growth for 1–2 days to reach 70–90% confluence in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 10% PBS, 2 mmol L−1 glutamin, 100 units per mL penicillin, and 100 units per mL streptomycin. The cells were then used in fluorescence imaging experiments. In details, after washing the cells three times by DMEM, 2 mL DMEM containing carboxyl functionalized PFBT probe were added to the cell culture and, afterward, the cells were incubated for another 30 min in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air at 37 °C. For the copper(II) ions treated samples, the cells were prewashed twice, and incubated with 10 μmol L−1 of 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid doped PFBT in DMEM for 30 min at 37 °C. After that, the cells were washed three times to remove extracellular free probes, then 50 μmol L−1 Cu2+ was added and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. For samples treated with His, the same procedures were performed except for an extra step in which, after incubation with copper ions, the cells were washed twice, and then were incubated with 250 μmol L−1 His for further 30 min. All the cells were washed twice with PBS followed by addition of 1 mL DMEM culture medium before observation under a fluorescence microscope.

PDs were prepared by the nanoprecipitation technique, in which the rapid mixing of polymers in THF solution with water resulted in dot formation, driven by hydrophobic interactions [35]. The synthesized PDs were then characterized by TEM, DLS, zeta potential measurements, and FTIR spectroscopy ( Fig. 1). The TEM image (Fig. 1a) reveals that the PDs are roughly spherical shape with an average diameter of about 18 nm; this result was further confirmed by DLS analysis shown in Fig. 1b, in which the average hydrodynamic diameter is 25 nm. It is clear that the hydrodynamic diameter of the PDs obtained by DLS should be larger than their size carried out by TEM, because the hydrodynamic diameter is the hydrated diameter of PDs cores together with the solvent coating layer [35]. Based on the zeta potential measurements (Fig. 1c), the bare PDs possessed a negatively charged surface with a zeta potential of −15 mV; however, after functionalization with 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid, the surface charge was increased to approximately −43 mV, confirming that the nanoparticles surface has been functionalized with carboxylic moieties (Fig. 1d).

FTIR spectroscopy was also utilized to characterize the structure and surface groups of the PDs (see Fig. 1e and Fig. S1). As seen, the FTIR spectrum of PD shows a broad and strong peak between 3000 and 3700 cm−1, which is assigned to the O-H stretching vibrations. The existence of C˭O groups in the PDs is confirmed from 1640 cm−1 peak. Besides, the peaks at 2926.3 and 2855.1 cm−1 are attributed to the aromatic and aliphatic C-H groups, respectively. The peak at 1048.3 cm−1 is also corresponded to the C-N stretching vibration, which is in resonance with carbon–carbon double bond. Thus, based on the information obtained by FTIR spectroscopy, it can be concluded that the 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid is doped successfully on the PDs.

To further study the optical properties of the PDs, their UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were obtained at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 2a. As seen, the PDs show a broad absorption band ranging from 300 to 540 nm that is suitable for laser excitation and fluorescence microscopy. The PDs possess a strong yellow emission centered at 540 nm when it was excited at a wavelength of 460 nm. Additionally, the dispersion of PDs exhibited a strong yellow emission under UV lamp illumination (365 nm), as shown in the inset of Fig. 2a. The PL quantum yield of the PDs in water was calculated to be 25%, in comparison with fluorescein (Φ = 0.925 in 0.1 M NaOH aqueous solution) and, thus, seems to be quite useful for acting as highly sensitive nanoprobes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing behavior of PDs towards metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In preliminary experiments, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of PDs is significantly quenched in the presence of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions. Meanwhile, only in the case of Cu2+ ion, the quenched fluorescence of the modified PDs found to significantly restore in the presence of His. Thus, considering the outstanding properties of the PDs including high photoluminesence quantum yield and water solubility, we evaluated the feasibility of the fluorescent nanoprobe for direct detection of Fe3+ ion and for quantitative detection and Cu2+-His system, as an “off-on” fluorescent sensor. Furthermore, the experimental conditions such as response time, pH of solution, and concentration of PDs were investigated and optimized for the detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+-His.

To study the response time of the PDs to Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of the PDs with Cu2+ was measured over different times. Fig. 2b shows the time dependent fluorescence quenching of the PDs in the presence of Cu2+. The results indicated that the fluorescence quenching is reached a constant value within 2 min, which implied a fast quenching kinetics of the system. A similar behavior was also observed in the presence of Fe3+ ions. Such a short response time of the PDs towards the presence of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions can be ascribed to the facilitated absorption of the metal ions to the carboxylic acid decorated surface of the modified PDs, rather than a merely pure thermodynamic based phenomenon such as coordinative tendency [40,41]. To further clarifying the basis for such experimentally obtained results, we compared the UV–vis spectra of the modified PDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ion and the results are shown in Fig. S2. As is obvious, there are 2 absorption peaks at about 320 and 450 nm for the yellow color solution of the 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid doped PFBT PDs, more or less similar to that previously reported for the PFBT PDs [42,43]. However, the addition of Cu2+ ion to the PDs solution resulted in no measurable change in its UV–vis spectrum, which indicates that the coordination tendency does not play a predominant role in fluorescence behavior of the PDs towards the Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in solution.

Furthermore, the fluorescence responses of PDs at different pH values from 3.0 to 9.0 were investigated and the results are shown in Fig. S2. The results indicated that the PDs exhibit a considerable fluorescence quenching effect upon addition of Cu2+ (1.5 µmol L−1) in both weak acidity and weak alkalinity. Thus, a biological pH of 7.4, using a 10 mmol L−1 phosphate buffer solution, was selected for both off and on studies. A similar trend was also observed for the case of Fe3+ ion.

Under optimized experimental conditions, the sensitivity of PDs for Cu2+ and Fe3+ were then examined. As can be seen from Fig. 2c and e, the fluorescence intensity of PDs at 540 nm decreased gradually with increasing Cu2+ and Fe3+ concentrations, respectively, indicating that the fluorescent nanoprobe is quite sensitive to both cations. Good correlation coefficients of R2 = 0.950 and 0.984 were evaluated for the Cu2+concentration changes over the ranges of 0.1–2.0 and 2–600 µmol L−1, respectively (Fig. 2d), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 61.7 nmol L−1 (based on a signal to noise ratio of 3). In the case of Fe3+ ion, the correlation coefficients of R2 = 0.961 and 0.991 were obtained over the concentration ranges of 0.1–2 and 2–720 µmol L−1, respectively (Fig. 2f), with a LOD of 58.1 nmol L−1. Considering the LOD of designed sensors, which is below the maximum level permitted by the US Environmental Protection Agency, and in the limit permitted by the European Union (0.2 µmol L−1), the designed nanosensor is quite promising for monitoring the concentrations of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in water and serum samples [44]. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for 5 replicates of 2.0 µmol L−1 Cu2+and Fe3+ ions were found to be 6.45% and 6.11%, respectively.

In order to explore the selectivity of the PDs for Cu2+ and Fe3+ions, the fluorescence response of the PDs at 540 nm was recorded after the introduction of various competitive metal ions (200 µmol L−1) including Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+,Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+ and Al3+. As can be seen from Fig. 3a and b, while the addition of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions results in a significant fluorescence quenching, the other cations produce negligible effects on the fluorescence intensity of PDs. The good selectivity could be ascribed by the high affinity of -COOH groups at the PDs surface towards the Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions [38]. We also observed that, at a pH of 7.4, the addition of EDTA, as a strong chelating agent, could fully restore the fluorescence of PDs for Cu2+ ions, while for Fe3+ ions, no measurable restoring happens even at excessive concentration of EDTA. It should be noted that this observation was despite the fact that the bonding constant of the EDTA complex with Fe3+ as a triply charged cation is more than that with a doubly charged cation like Cu2+. However, it is well known that the solution pH has an important role on the Fe–EDTA chelation and Fe hydrolysis [45]. The dissociation constants for Fe-EDTA chelates increased between pH 7.4 and 9.0, mainly due to the formation of mixed EDTA-hydroxy chelates FeEDTA(OH)2 with more rapid dissociation kinetics. In addition, the solubility of Fe3+ is very low in neutral or alkaline pHs, due to the formation of insoluble Fe(OH)3. Hence, by subtracting the fluorescence enhancement after addition of EDTA, the concentration of copper in solution can be easily calculated.

Since Cu2+ can be strongly and selectively coordinated to His, to form a very stable complex, the presence of this amino acid in a solution containing PDs/Cu2+ causes the removal of Cu2+ from the surface of PDs into solution, and resulting in the restoration of the quenched fluorescence of PDs [22]. Therefore, the PDs/Cu2+can be used as a potential turn-on fluorescent sensor for His. To examine this, first, the solution of PDs was treated with enough Cu2+ ions (i.e., 600 µmol L−1) to acquire an effectively quenched fluorescence of the PDs/Cu2+nanoprobe. Then, different concentration of His were added to restore the fluorescence of PDs. Fig. 4a shows that the quenched fluorescence signal of the PDs is significantly enhanced with the increasing concentrations of His. As can be seen from Fig. 4b, under the optimized experimental conditions, two linear responses are obtained over the range of 0.1–920 µmol L−1; one from 0.1 to 2.0 µmol L−1 (R2 = 0.972) and the other from 2 to 920 µmol L−1 (R2 = 0.970), with a LOD of 79.6 nmol L−1 for His (at S/N = 3). It is worth mentioning that the presence of His could also be readily observed under UV-illumination (see inset of Fig. 4a). The RSD for five replicate detections of a 25 µmol L−1 concentration of His was found to be 5.47%, implying a satisfactory precision.

In the next step, the effects of various amino acids including cysteine, serine, phenylalanine, tyrosine, threonine, leucine, alanine, methionine, asparagine, and arginine on the performance of His assay at a concentration of 100 µmol L−1 were examined separately and the results are shown in Fig. 4c and d. As clearly seen, the other amino acids have quite low interfering effects on the fluorescence restoring of the PDs/Cu2+ nanoprobe, compared to that occurred in the presence of His, which suggests its potential as a highly selective fluorescent nanoprobe for His detection. It is interesting to note that the thiol-containing amino acids such as cysteine or homocysteine that can form Cu-S bonds with copper ions have negligible effect on the fluorescence restoring of PDs/Cu2+ nanoprobe at a neutral pH. This observation can be related to different acid dissociation constant (pKa) of these amino acids. The pKa values of histidine and cysteine are 6 and 8, respectively. Therefore, some 75% of the side chain of cysteine is protonated at pH 7.4 [22,46]. As a result, the attachment of H+ to binding sites of cysteine can lead to the reduction of its tendency to complex formation with copper(II) at neutral pH and, consequently, cannot compete with histidine for the copper ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition mechanism for off-on switching behavior of 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid @PFBT PDs
ce:section.ce:para: Originally, our results revealed that the re-dispersion occurred upon addition of His to the PDs/Cu2+ nanoprobe, which leads to the fluorescence recovery, as reported in some of metal-complex probes for secondary recognition [47]. Transmission electron microscopy and dynamic light scattering measurements were then used to explore the recognition mechanism before and after addition of His to PDs/Cu2+ nanoprobe. As shown in Fig. 5, the diameter of as prepared PDs was about 20 nm on average (Fig. 5a) that reached to about 600 nm after the addition of Cu2+ (Fig. 5c), implying that the functionalized PDs results in a strong aggregation induced florescence quenching in the presence of Cu2+ ions. However, there was no observable changes in UV–Vis spectra of modified PFBT PDs before and after addition of Cu2+ compared to bare PFBT PDs (Fig. S3). This is mainly due to the high tendency of -COOH groups present at the modified PDs for complexation with copper ions, via formation of a sandwich complex between COOH groups on the surface of PDs and Cu2+ ions [38]. Then, the aggregated PDs will be re-dispersed upon addition of His and removal of the copper ions from the PDs surfaces (Fig. 5e). The results were also confirmed by the corresponding DLS measurements shown in Fig. 5b, d and f. These results are in support of the selective affinity of His to Cu2+, which results in removal of Cu2+ from its PDs complex [48].

As it shown in Fig. 6a, it was interestingly found that the observed aggregation-disaggregation process could be repeated for several times, indicating the possibility of reuse of the prepared nanoprobe for His detection. However, after some switching cycles, the fluorescence intensity gradually diminished, which can be related to the nature of the close competition process between the PDs and His for copper ions in solution [24]. Notably, the response time for both aggregation and dispersion of PDs was less than one min, which is quite short, so that, the fluorescence intensity does not significantly change, even after several days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of COOH functionalized PFBT PDstohistidine-rich proteins, biological samples and living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Albumins, such as Bovine serum albumin (BSA), have abundant His residues [49]. In the presence of 2.0 equivalent of BSA, the fluorescence intensity of PDs/Cu2+ at 540 nm was increased significantly (Fig. 6b), indicating that this probe has high selectivity toward His residues on protein surfaces.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of the proposed turn-on fluorescent probe to detect His in biological fluids, different concentrations of His were spiked to human serum and human plasma samples. As shown in Table 1, the results for different spiked concentrations are in satisfactory agreement with those obtained by dispersive derivatization liquid–liquid microextraction (DDLLME) combined with GC-MS method [50]. Besides, the His recoveries from both human serum and plasma samples are between acceptable values of 90.0% and 96.8%.

We also used MCF-7 to demonstrate the applicability of the proposed nanosensing system to living cells. As is clearly seen from Fig. 7a-c, loading of 10 µmol L−1 of PDs into the MCF-7 cell medium resulted in an intense yellow fluorescence. This result manifests that the prepared nanoprobe possesses excellent cell permeability, and is quite suitable for live cell imaging. Interestingly, after incubating the cells with 50 µmol L−1Cu2+ for another 30 min, the fluorescence intensity remarkably decreased due to aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching mechanism (Fig. 7d-f). Meanwhile, the florescence signal recovers by further treatment of the cells with 250 µmol L−1Hisfor 30 min incubation (Fig. 7g-i). This observation confirmed that the PDs/Cu2+aggregate possesses a promising potential to monitor the His level in living cells.

However, as it is seen from Fig. 7h, the fluorescence intensity of the PDs/Cu2+/His seems somewhat stronger than that of the initial PDs. This could be mainly due to the fact that most of the live cells naturally contain traces of copper and other metal ions. Thus, a possible reason for some stronger fluorescence of PDs/Cu2+/His, compared to free PDs might be due to the interaction of such naturally available copper ions inside the cells with PDs. A further reason for such observation might be related to the high population of cells present and their possible stacking, as can also be seen from the corresponding images.

Therefore, the PDs/Cu2+ probe could be extended as a valuable fluorescent probe to detect Hisrich proteins, and His content of biological fluids, and to use for intracellular His imaging. We also performed the MTT test to see viability of MCF-7 cell against different concentration of theprobe, copper and His and the results are shown in Fig. S4. These results manifested the high biocompatibility and low cytotoxic activity for the cells even at high concentrations, astheviability remained above ca. 80% after 24 h incubation time, revealing that 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid@PFBT PDs are biocompatible and possess high potential to use in living cells

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid doped PFBT PDs with previously reported fluorescent probes for detection of copper ion and His
ce:section.ce:para: In Table 2, the linear dynamic range (LDR) and limit of detection (LOD) of some previously reportedfor determination ofCu2+ ion [22,24,40,51,53,57,58], His [22,24,45,53–56,59], and Fe3+ ion [25–28,60] Cu2+ ion [22,24,40,52,56,57], His [22,24,45,52–55,58], and Fe3+ ion [25–28,58] in different real samples fluorescent probes are compared with those obtained in the present study. As is quite obvious from Table 2, except with few minor cases, the figures of merit of the proposed off-on nanoprobe for copper ion and His are superior over most of the other reports. Furthermore, the carboxyl functionalized PFBT PDs in this work possess such advantages as low cost, easy synthesis, low toxicity, and strong photoluminescence, compared to most of the previously used fluorescent probes including modified carbon dots [51,58,60], nanoclusters [22,25,40,53], quantum dots [44,56] and G-quadruplex/Cu2+ switches [45].

In this work, we synthesized and fully characterized the carboxyl functionalized PFBT PDs using 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid. In the presence of water, carboxyl moiety of 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid hydrolysis and creates negatively charged polymer dots to form water soluble nanoprobes. It was demonstrated that the yellow emitting PDs, with no need to further surface modification, can be utilized as a turn-off fluorescence sensor for selective detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions based on aggregation induced fluorescence quenching mechanism. Besides, In the presence of EDTA chelating molecules, at a pH of 7.4, the copper ions can be removed from the aggregates and restore the fluorescence, but this cannot happen to iron ions. Thus, the fluorescent nanoprobe can be applied to simultaneous determination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in solution. Furthermore, the observed reversible fluorescence recovery manifested that the PDs/Cu2+ aggregates have the potential to be used as a ‘‘turn-on’’ fluorescence biosensor for His detection. In fact, His can effectively shelter the fluorescence quenching due to sensitive and competitive complex formation with Cu2+ ion with the nanoprobes. Thus, considering the excellent properties of the PFBT/9-anthracenecarboxylic acid sensing probe including high stability, intense photoluminescence, water solubility and good photostability, the designed biosensor could be successfully applied to selective detection Fe3+, Cu2+, and His with low limit of detection and wide linear range in environmental and biological samples, and also possess the potential in cellular imaging. The developed biosensor exhibited promising opportunities for practical applications to biological and clinical diagnosis fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of this work by Iran Science Elites Federation (ISEF) and Research Councils of Tarbiat Modares and Razi Universities is gratefully acknowledged. We also thank Payam Noor University, Qom Branch, for the laboratory support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.10.072.
